team.name: Jacksonville Jaguars
team.notes: No 1st-round pick in 2026 (traded to Cleveland in Travis Hunter trade). First pick is No. 56 overall (2nd round). Four picks in top 100 including three 3rd-rounders.

gm.name: James Gladstone
gm.title: General Manager
gm.previous_role: Director of Scouting Strategy, Los Angeles Rams
gm.notes: Youngest GM in NFL at time of hire. Only one draft as GM (2025). Profile heavily reliant on Rams scouting tree and stated philosophy.

career_path.role: Media and Literacy Coordinator / Football Coach

career_path.mentor: N/A — pre-NFL career in education; coached Les Snead's son Logan, which led to NFL opportunity

career_path.role: Senior Assistant to the General Manager
Senior Assistant to the GM / Player Personnel Coordinator

career_path.mentor: Les Snead (GM); Rams won Super Bowl LVI this season
Les Snead (GM); credited with instrumental role in 2023-2024 draft classes (Puka Nacua, Jared Verse, Kobie Turner, Braden Fiske, Byron Young)

scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Pure Snead/McVay Rams tree. Snead's philosophy centers on: (1) film-first evaluation over combine testing, (2) willingness to trade draft capital aggressively for elite talent ('F--- Them Picks'), (3) mining later rounds for starters, (4) using free agency to avoid desperation drafting, (5) valuing production and intangibles. Gladstone spent his entire NFL career (9 years) under Snead and absorbed this system completely. The Rams did not attend the combine in person for 6 consecutive years; Gladstone adopted this same approach in his second year with Jacksonville.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: Gladstone has already shown one major departure from Snead's later approach: willingness to trade UP for a singular talent (Travis Hunter), whereas post-Super Bowl Snead pivoted to accumulating picks. However, this mirrors Snead's earlier aggressive trade-up mentality (Jared Goff No. 1 overall in 2016). Gladstone explicitly referenced the 'F--- Them Picks' mentality after the Hunter trade, suggesting he carries the full Rams DNA including the aggressive phase.
scouting_tree.cross_pollination: None. Gladstone's entire professional NFL career was with one organization (LA Rams). No competing philosophical influences.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: Only 1 draft as JAX GM. Rams lineage is the dominant signal. Gladstone has explicitly drawn parallels between the Jaguars' current situation and the Rams' 2017 inflection point. Weight should drop to 0.50 after 2026 draft, 0.30 after 2027.

key_influencers.name: Liam Coen
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Offensive-minded; comes from Rams coaching tree (2018-2020, 2022 with LAR) and Buccaneers OC (2024). Values offensive line depth, pass protection, balanced offenses. Strong scheme preferences on offense — wants versatile OL who can zone block. Defensive philosophy emphasizes zone coverage, rush four and play coverage behind it. Named finalist for 2025 AP Coach of the Year.
key_influencers.relationship_to_gm: Coen and Gladstone overlapped at the Rams from 2018-2020 and 2022. Strong pre-existing relationship and shared Rams philosophical DNA. Coen was hired before Gladstone.

key_influencers.name: Tony Boselli
key_influencers.title: Executive Vice President of Football Operations
key_influencers.known_biases: Pro Football Hall of Fame offensive tackle (Jaguars' first-ever draft pick, 1995). Reports directly to owner Shad Khan. Aided in the GM search that landed Gladstone. Likely influences OL evaluation with elite-level playing experience. Emphasized improving offensive and defensive lines as priority areas.
key_influencers.relationship_to_gm: Boselli was hired before Gladstone and was involved in selecting him. Reports to Khan, not Gladstone. Acts as organizational connective tissue between ownership and football operations.

key_influencers.name: Tony Khan
key_influencers.title: Chief Football Strategy Officer
key_influencers.known_biases: Analytics-driven; son of owner Shad Khan. Runs the team's analytics/statistical research department. Primary role is scouting undrafted free agents. Has publicly praised Hunter trade using analytics framing. Owns TruMedia Networks (sports analytics firm). Influence on draft-day picks appears limited relative to previous Baalke regime; Gladstone appears to have stronger autonomy.
key_influencers.relationship_to_gm: Part of the 'football leadership team' along with Gladstone, Coen, and Boselli. All four report to owner Shad Khan.

key_influencers.name: Anthony Campanile
key_influencers.title: Defensive Coordinator
key_influencers.known_biases: Came from Green Bay Packers (LBs coach/run game coordinator). Runs a zone-heavy defensive scheme emphasizing rush four and play coverage. Values versatile defenders. Returning for 2026 after interviewing for HC jobs.
key_influencers.relationship_to_gm: Campanile was hired by Coen before Gladstone was in place. Influences defensive player evaluations and scheme-fit assessments.

front_office.power_dynamics: Collaborative four-person leadership structure (Gladstone, Coen, Boselli, Tony Khan) all reporting to owner Shad Khan. Gladstone has final say on draft picks but Coen has significant input, especially on offensive personnel. Coen described watching Gladstone operate the draft room as 'unbelievable' and praised his communication. Boselli provides organizational perspective and OL expertise. Tony Khan contributes analytics lens. This is a new power structure with all principals in their first or second year in their roles.
front_office.owner_interference_notes: Shad Khan is directly involved in hiring decisions and structural organization. He initially retained Baalke after firing Pederson, then reversed course. Khan created the multi-headed leadership structure (GM, HC, EVP, CFSO all reporting to him). Tony Khan's analytics role represents ongoing family involvement. However, Khan appears to trust Gladstone on football decisions — the Hunter trade was Gladstone's call.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Prioritize 'intangibly rich' players who elevate the ecosystem by being themselves. Film-first evaluation. Production matters more than combine numbers. Trust sourced intel over short pre-draft visits. Willing to be bold and aggressive when a franchise-altering player is available.
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: In his one draft, Gladstone traded up aggressively for the consensus best player (BPA) who also happened to address needs (WR and CB). Day 2 picks addressed clear needs (secondary, OL). Day 3 targeted versatile, high-character players with special teams value. Rams lineage suggests a hybrid approach: use free agency to fill holes, then draft BPA. Snead explicitly stated the Rams' macro philosophy is to 'use free agency to not be desperate in the draft.' Gladstone appears to follow this — he made significant FA signings at OL before drafting OL in round 3.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Gladstone says he'll draft BPA, but the Hunter trade was simultaneously the best player AND a massive need-fill at two positions. Hard to distinguish BPA from need with only one draft. Rams lineage under Snead showed flexibility — Snead admitted the 2025 Rams draft was 'more specific in terms of positions we were looking for' rather than pure BPA.

trade_triggers: Generational/franchise-altering talent available within trading range
Player that Gladstone views as capable of 'altering the trajectory of the sport'
Opportunity to accumulate Day 2/3 picks when value isn't available at current position (traded down on Day 2 with Detroit to acquire future 3rd-rounders)
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Aggressive and flexible. Gladstone was schooled in the Rams' 'F--- Them Picks' era where Snead routinely traded away first-rounders for proven veterans and draft assets. Gladstone explicitly referenced this mentality. In his first draft, he traded up for Hunter (giving up a future 1st, current 2nd, current 4th) but also traded down on Day 2 to accumulate future picks. Comfortable operating without first-round picks — the Rams went 7 consecutive years without one during Gladstone's tenure. Gladstone values volume of mid-round picks, citing the Rams' success with 24 picks over two draft cycles.

trade_history.description: Traded No. 5 overall, No. 36 (2nd round), No. 126 (4th round), and 2026 1st-round pick to Cleveland for No. 2 overall, No. 104 (4th round), and No. 200 (6th round). Significant overpay by trade chart metrics to select Travis Hunter.
trade_history.trigger: Generational two-way talent in Travis Hunter

trade_history.description: Traded down from No. 70 to No. 102 with Detroit Lions, acquiring two 2026 3rd-round picks. Accumulated future mid-round capital.
trade_history.trigger: Value accumulation; no target at 70 worth staying for

trade_history.description: Traded into 3rd round to acquire picks 88 and 89 (Caleb Ransaw and Wyatt Milum)
trade_history.trigger: Targeted players at positions of need (S/CB, OL)

position_avoidance: QB — franchise QB Trevor Lawrence already in place on long-term deal. No need to draft QB.
position_premium: CB/secondary — worst pass defense in NFL in 2024, addressed with Hunter at No. 2 and Ransaw at No. 88. OL depth — explicitly stated as priority by Gladstone. EDGE depth — drafted McLeod (Rd 6) to address lack of 3rd edge rusher.
positional_value.notes: Only one draft to draw from. Rams lineage suggests premium on defensive front (Verse, Turner, Fiske, Young all DL/EDGE under Gladstone's scouting tenure) and offensive playmakers (Nacua, Kupp). Gladstone's Rams rarely invested early in RB (Kyren Williams was a 5th-rounder) or TE.

QB.archetype_summary: Not applicable for 2026 draft — Trevor Lawrence is the franchise QB under long-term contract. If Gladstone were to evaluate QBs, Rams lineage suggests preference for pocket passers with enough mobility to operate in a McVay/Coen-style play-action heavy offense. Coen's Tampa Bay offense featured Baker Mayfield, a mobile pocket passer with quick processing. Lawrence fits this mold — strong arm, improving pocket presence, some mobility.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Arm strength and processing speed prioritized over 40 time based on Rams lineage (Jared Goff, Matthew Stafford were pocket-first)
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a primary factor; pocket mobility matters more than straight-line speed

playing_style.primary_style: Pocket passer with play-action acumen and enough mobility to extend plays. Coen's system demands quick processing, accuracy on intermediate routes, and ability to execute rollout/bootleg concepts.
most_valued_traits: Arm strength
Processing speed and pre-snap reads
Intermediate accuracy (10-20 yards)
Pocket presence and pressure navigation
Play-action execution
least_important_traits: Designed rushing ability — Coen's system uses QB movement by design but doesn't rely on scrambling
deal_breakers: Run-first QBs with limited processing ability
Inaccurate intermediate throwers

college_production.production_floor: No data — inferred from Rams: must have been a multi-year starter

examples: No QBs drafted by Gladstone. Rams under Snead/Gladstone drafted Stetson Bennett (Rd 4, 2023) as a developmental project — suggesting willingness to take late-round QB fliers but not invest premium capital when franchise QB is in place.

QB.confidence_note: No QBs drafted. Profile entirely inferred from Rams lineage and current roster context. Irrelevant for 2026 draft.

EDGE.archetype_summary: Versatile, productive pass rusher who can stand up or put hand in dirt. Rams lineage strongly favors high-motor, productive college pass rushers with positional flexibility. Jared Verse (Rd 1, 2024, 6-4, 247, 4.58) and Byron Young (Rd 3, 2023, 6-3, 249) exemplify the Rams model: length, motor, and versatility over pure speed. Gladstone drafted Jalen McLeod (Rd 6, Auburn, 22.5 career sacks) — a tweener LB/EDGE with production, motor, and positional versatility.

physical_profile.height_range: 6-1 to 6-5 — Rams have shown willingness to draft undersized EDGE (Josaiah Stewart at 6-1 in 2025). Jared Verse at 6-4 is more typical.
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum based on Rams history — Stewart at 6-1 shows flexibility on length. However, Verse (33.5-inch arms) represents the prototype.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Rams are film-first and don't attend the combine in person. Vertical and broad jump as explosiveness proxies likely matter, but combine numbers are confirmatory, not eliminatory.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.55-4.70 range acceptable. Verse ran 4.58. Not looking for 4.3 speed rushers.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Motor, effort, and relentless pursuit. Rams EDGEs under Gladstone's scouting win with effort, power, and a diverse rush repertoire rather than pure speed or bend.
most_valued_traits: Relentless motor and effort (non-negotiable)
Pass rush move diversity/toolbox
Positional versatility (stand-up or hand-in-dirt)
College production — must have demonstrated ability to get to the QB
Ability to set the edge in run defense
least_important_traits: Elite first-step explosion — nice to have but not required if motor and technique compensate
deal_breakers: Low motor/effort players who take plays off
One-dimensional rushers who cannot contribute in run defense
Players with extensive injury history limiting availability
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Exceptional motor and versatility combined with ascending production trajectory

college_production.production_floor: McLeod had 22.5 career sacks (8 in final year). Verse had 17 career sacks at FSU. Minimum 5+ sacks in final college season for Day 2 consideration.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending production — McLeod's sack totals went 1, 2.5, 5.5, 8 across his career. Verse transferred from Albany to FSU and improved each year.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Gladstone values production in context. Rams evaluated players from diverse levels (Verse from Albany→FSU, McLeod from App State→Auburn).

examples: 2025 Rd 6 (194) — Jalen McLeod, Auburn/App State: 22.5 career sacks, 8 in final year. LB/EDGE tweener who projects as edge defender in NFL. High motor, versatile, productive.
rams_lineage_examples: 2024 Rd 1 (19) — Jared Verse, Florida State: 6-4, 247, 4.58 40. Led all rookies in pressures (77). Power/motor rusher with diverse moves.
2023 Rd 3 (77) — Byron Young, Tennessee: 6-3, 249. High motor, versatile stand-up rusher.

EDGE.confidence_note: Only 1 EDGE drafted (Day 3). Heavily supplemented by Rams lineage data.

OT.archetype_summary: Athletic, versatile offensive lineman with tackle-guard flexibility and experience in zone-blocking concepts. Values grit, toughness, and OL depth over pure premium athleticism. Wyatt Milum (Rd 3, 2025) was a 4-year starting LT at WVU (6-6, 317) drafted to play guard — shows Gladstone values versatility and is willing to move players inside. Rams lineage includes Steve Avila (Rd 2, 2023, guard who moved to center) and Rob Havenstein (long-tenured RT).

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum evident — Milum's tackle-to-guard conversion suggests arm length matters less than technique and versatility
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Rams/Gladstone are film-first. Lateral agility and ability to operate in zone-blocking concepts matter. Combine numbers are secondary.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a primary factor for OT evaluation

playing_style.primary_style: Versatile zone-blocking lineman who can play multiple positions along the line. Coen's offense requires OL who can reach-block, pull, and operate in a zone-run scheme.
most_valued_traits: Positional versatility — ability to play tackle and guard
Toughness and grit (Coen praised Milum's 'gritty attitude')
Balance and strong hands at the point of attack
Zone-blocking ability and lateral movement
OL depth value — Gladstone explicitly said 'you can never have enough security across the offensive line'
least_important_traits: Elite combine testing — Rams/Gladstone don't chase athletic OL measurables
deal_breakers: Stiff, phone-booth-only blockers who can't operate in space
Players without multi-position experience or flexibility
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Tackle-guard versatility combined with Senior Bowl or all-star game performance (Milum was praised for Senior Bowl work at guard)

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter preferred. Milum started all 4 years at WVU (initially at RT, then LT). 2nd-team AP All-American, Big 12 OL of the Year.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers players who started early and stayed consistent. Ascending trajectory preferred.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Values players from physical conferences (Big 12, SEC, etc.) but Rams have also mined smaller schools.

examples: 2025 Rd 3 (89) — Wyatt Milum, West Virginia: 6-6, 317. 4-year starter at LT. Big 12 OL of the Year. Moved to guard for Jaguars. Praised for grit and versatility.
2025 Rd 7 (221) — Jonah Monheim, USC: Center. 3rd-team All-Big Ten. Depth center behind Robert Hainsey.

OT.confidence_note: 2 OL drafted (Rd 3 and Rd 7). Milum is the primary archetype evidence. Supplemented by Coen's offensive scheme requirements and Rams OL philosophy.

IOL.archetype_summary: Versatile interior linemen who can play center and guard, operate in zone-blocking concepts, and provide positional depth. Gladstone explicitly prioritizes OL depth. Jonah Monheim (Rd 7, 2025, USC) was a center drafted for backup/depth role. Rams lineage includes Steve Avila (Rd 2, 2023, guard→center), showing a preference for IOL who can slide between positions.

physical_profile.height_range: 6-2 to 6-6 (Milum at 6-6 playing guard shows willingness to draft taller guards)
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum — IOL evaluation appears technique-focused
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Lateral agility and ability to pull/reach in zone scheme. Film-first evaluation.

playing_style.primary_style: Zone-blocking guard or center with position versatility. Must be able to get to the second level and sustain blocks in space.
most_valued_traits: Center-guard versatility
Zone-blocking footwork and lateral movement
Anchor strength at point of attack
Intelligence and communication (especially for centers)
Toughness and durability
least_important_traits: Pure power/mauler traits in a phone-booth — Coen's scheme asks more for movement than pure anchor
deal_breakers: Players who can only play one IOL position
Poor lateral movement that won't translate to zone scheme
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite versatility (ability to play all three interior positions)

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter preferred. Monheim started at center for USC.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Conference quality matters for evaluating competition faced

examples: 2025 Rd 3 (89) — Wyatt Milum, West Virginia: 6-6, 317. Drafted as OT, transitioned to guard. Versatile.
2025 Rd 7 (221) — Jonah Monheim, USC: Center. Backup depth piece behind Hainsey.
rams_lineage_examples: 2023 Rd 2 (36) — Steve Avila, TCU: Guard who moved to center in 2024. All-Rookie team. Epitomizes Rams IOL versatility preference.

IOL.confidence_note: 2 OL drafted with IOL projection. Rams lineage (Avila) provides stronger archetype signal.

DT.archetype_summary: Disruptive, high-motor interior defender who can penetrate and collapse the pocket. Rams lineage is extremely strong here — Kobie Turner (Rd 3, 2023, Wake Forest, 6-1, 290), Braden Fiske (Rd 2, 2024, Florida State, 6-3, 295), and Aaron Donald (Rd 1, 2014) all share an undersized, penetrating, high-motor archetype. The Rams' last 5 defensive interior players drafted were all under 300 lbs. Gladstone did not draft a DT in 2025 but DT is a clear 2026 need.

physical_profile.weight_range: 275-300 — Rams consistently draft DTs under 300 lbs, valuing quickness over mass
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum — Turner at 6-1 shows willingness to draft smaller DTs
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: First-step quickness and explosiveness. 3-cone drill and short shuttle likely valued for interior quickness. Film-first evaluation.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not about 40 time — about get-off and quickness in short area

playing_style.primary_style: Penetrating, disruptive interior pass rusher. Undersized, quick 3-technique rather than two-gap nose tackle. The Rams under Snead/Gladstone value interior disruption (the Aaron Donald legacy).
most_valued_traits: First-step quickness and explosion off the snap
Relentless motor (consistent across all Rams DL evaluations)
Pass rush ability from the interior — sack production matters
Ability to disrupt in the backfield (TFLs, pressures)
Positional versatility along the defensive front
least_important_traits: Raw size — Rams have repeatedly shown willingness to play lighter DTs
Two-gap ability — scheme asks DTs to penetrate, not hold the point
deal_breakers: Heavy, immobile nose tackles who lack pass rush upside
Players without demonstrated college pass rush production
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite interior pass rush ability, especially from undersized players who compensate with quickness and technique

college_production.production_floor: Must show pass rush production. Turner had 9 sacks in final college season. Fiske had 8.5. Minimum 5+ sacks or 8+ TFLs in final season for Day 2 consideration.
college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending production valued. Turner transferred from Wyoming to Wake Forest and elevated his game.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Rams mine all levels — Turner came from Wake Forest, not a blue-blood program. Production matters more than pedigree.

rams_lineage_examples: 2024 Rd 2 (52) — Braden Fiske, Florida State: 6-3, 295. DROY nominee. Penetrating interior rusher.
2023 Rd 3 (89) — Kobie Turner, Wake Forest: 6-1, 290. Started from Day 1. All-Rookie team. Quintessential undersized, quick DT.
2014 Rd 1 (13) — Aaron Donald, Pitt: 6-1, 285. The gold standard of the Rams DT archetype.

DT.confidence_note: No DTs drafted by Gladstone as JAX GM. Rams lineage is the entire signal and it's very strong/consistent.

CB.archetype_summary: Versatile, physical, ball-skilled defensive backs who can play multiple secondary positions. Gladstone's first pick (Travis Hunter at No. 2) was a CB/WR hybrid — the ultimate versatility play. His third pick (Caleb Ransaw, Rd 3, Tulane) was described as a 'do-everything type' with slot and outside experience. The new Jacksonville defense under Campanile emphasizes zone coverage, not press-man, which means scheme demands versatile DBs who can tackle, play zone, and contribute to run support rather than pure man-coverage specialists.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum — Hunter's 31 3/8-inch arms are average. Versatility matters more than length.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed matters but Gladstone didn't see Hunter's 40 time before drafting him (Hunter skipped combine testing). Film-first. Tackling ability explicitly valued — Gladstone praised Ransaw's 'tackle ratio.'
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.32-4.45 range acceptable based on limited data (Hunter ran 4.39 at pro day; Ransaw's speed undisclosed but described as 'fast')

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Zone-based with versatility to play man. Campanile's scheme runs quarters/zone-shell coverage, rush four and play coverage behind it. Not a pure press-man scheme.
most_valued_traits: Positional versatility — ability to play outside CB, slot, and potentially safety
Physicality and tackling ability (Gladstone explicitly praised Ransaw's tackling)
Ball skills and forced incompletion rate (Ransaw's 26.1% forced incompletion rate noted)
Run support and willingness to come downhill
Football intelligence and instincts in zone coverage
least_important_traits: Pure press-man technique — the scheme doesn't demand it as the primary coverage tool
deal_breakers: Poor tacklers or players who avoid contact in run support
One-dimensional players who can only play outside or only play slot
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite ball skills and versatility across multiple secondary positions — Hunter was drafted specifically because he could play WR AND CB

college_production.production_floor: No strict statistical floor. Hunter had 7 INTs in two years at Colorado. Ransaw had 5 PBUs and 2 FFs. Film and tackling metrics valued more than raw interception totals.
college_production.production_trajectory: Multi-year starters preferred but not required (Hunter was an early-entry junior with only 2 years at Colorado).
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Gladstone values sourced intel and context over raw numbers.

examples: 2025 Rd 1 (2) — Travis Hunter, Colorado: 6-0, 188, 31 3/8-inch arms, 4.39 40. Heisman Trophy winner. Two-way player: 96 catches/1,258 yards/15 TDs on offense; 35 tackles/4 INTs/11 PBUs on defense in 2024. The ultimate versatility pick.

2025 Rd 3 (88) — Caleb Ransaw, Tulane: 6-0, 196. Described as versatile, physical DB with slot and outside experience. 26.1% forced incompletion rate. Gladstone praised his tackling. PFF projected him as a safety who could play nickel.

CB.confidence_note: 2 secondary players drafted (Hunter as CB/WR, Ransaw as versatile DB). Strong pattern of valuing versatility and physicality. Scheme context provides additional signal.

S.archetype_summary: Physical, versatile safeties who can tackle reliably, play multiple secondary positions, and contribute on special teams. Ransaw (Rd 3) projects as a safety in the NFL despite being listed as a DB. Rayuan Lane III (Rd 6, Navy) was drafted as a safety/special teamer. Both picks share common traits: physicality, tackling, versatility, and character. The Rams lineage includes Quentin Lake (Rd 6, 2022, UCLA) and Russ Yeast (Rd 7, 2022) — development safeties drafted late.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Range and ability to cover ground. Tackling metrics appear to matter significantly to Gladstone. Film-first evaluation.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.40-4.55 acceptable range based on Rams lineage

playing_style.primary_style: Versatile, physical safety who can play single-high or come into the box. Must be a reliable tackler first and foremost.
most_valued_traits: Reliable tackling — Gladstone literally said 'the root word of the position safety is safe' regarding Ransaw's ability to get ballcarriers down
Positional versatility across the secondary
Special teams value (Lane was described as a 'special teams warrior')
Football IQ and experience (Lane played 49 games at Navy)
Physicality in run support
least_important_traits: Elite deep-range speed — tackling and versatility prioritized over single-high range
deal_breakers: Poor tacklers or arm tacklers who miss in the open field
Players without special teams utility (especially for Day 3 picks)
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite tackle rate combined with ball-hawking instincts

college_production.production_floor: Lane had 244 career tackles, 21 PBUs, 7 INTs across 49 games at Navy. Ransaw had consistent tackle numbers. Extensive game experience valued.
college_production.production_trajectory: Multi-year starters with ascending or consistent production. Lane played 49 games — Gladstone values experience.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Gladstone drafted a Navy safety — he clearly doesn't discriminate based on school prestige. Production and character matter.

examples: 2025 Rd 3 (88) — Caleb Ransaw, Tulane: 6-0, 196. Listed as DB but projects as safety. Physical tackler with ball skills. 26.1% forced incompletion rate.
2025 Rd 6 (200) — Rayuan Lane III, Navy: Safety/special teamer. 244 career tackles, 21 PBUs, 7 INTs in 49 games. Self-described 'special teams warrior.'

S.confidence_note: 2 secondary players with safety projection. Clear pattern: physicality, tackling, versatility, experience, and character.

LB.archetype_summary: High-character, high-experience linebackers who can contribute immediately on special teams and develop into starters. Jack Kiser (Rd 4, Notre Dame, 6-2, 225) is the prototype: school record for games played (70), team captain, Man of the Year, outstanding special teams reputation. Not a pure athletic freak — a smart, dependable, high-floor player who embodies 'intangibly rich.' Rams lineage includes Byron Young (Rd 3, 2023) who was an immediate starter.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Film-first. Sideline-to-sideline speed matters but isn't measured purely by 40 time. Instincts and football IQ valued highly.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No strict speed threshold — Kiser is not an elite athlete but an elite football player

playing_style.primary_style: Smart, reliable, do-it-all linebacker who excels on special teams and can develop into a starter. Values instincts, experience, and character over pure athleticism.
most_valued_traits: Special teams excellence (Kiser's best shot as a rookie was special teams)
Extensive college experience and leadership (Kiser played 70 games, set school record)
Tackling reliability and consistency
High character and intangibles — team captain, community involvement, Wuerffel Trophy finalist
Ability to play multiple LB positions
least_important_traits: Elite 40 time or combine athleticism — Gladstone is clearly willing to draft LBs who project more on football IQ than physical tools
deal_breakers: Character concerns — Gladstone explicitly prioritizes 'intangibly rich' players
Players who cannot contribute on special teams (especially for Day 2-3 picks)
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Exceptional character, leadership, and college experience combined with ascending production

college_production.production_floor: Kiser: career-high 90 tackles in final season, 2 sacks, 5 TFLs. Not elite production but consistent and reliable. McLeod (EDGE/LB tweener): 22.5 career sacks. Minimum appears to be consistent tackling numbers.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values ascending production and durability. Kiser went from reserve to All-Sun Belt to starter at Auburn. McLeod's sack totals ascended each year.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Values players who transferred and succeeded at higher levels (McLeod: App State→Auburn). Also values players from elite programs who contributed for years (Kiser at Notre Dame).

examples: 2025 Rd 4 (107) — Jack Kiser, Notre Dame: 6-2, ~225. 70 career games (school record). Team captain. 90 tackles, 5 TFLs, 2 sacks in final year. Notre Dame Man of the Year. Special teams standout.
2025 Rd 6 (194) — Jalen McLeod, Auburn/App State: LB/EDGE tweener. 22.5 career sacks (8 in final year). Projects as edge defender in NFL.

LB.confidence_note: 2 LBs drafted (Rd 4 and Rd 6). Clear pattern of valuing character, experience, special teams ability, and ascending production from transfers.

WR.archetype_summary: Dynamic playmakers with elite ball skills, versatility, and the ability to impact the game in multiple ways. Travis Hunter (Rd 1) is the ultimate WR archetype for this regime — not a one-trick deep threat but a complete playmaker with elite ball skills who can also play defense. Rams lineage is exceptionally strong at WR evaluation: Puka Nacua (Rd 5, 2023, BYU, 6-2, 203) and Cooper Kupp (Rd 3, 2017, Eastern Washington, 6-2, 208) are the gold standard — productive college receivers from non-blue-blood programs who excelled on film despite modest athletic testing. Route running, ball skills, and football IQ over raw speed.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum — Hunter at 31 3/8-inch arms is below average
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Film-first. Rams famously don't attend the combine. Gladstone drafted Hunter without seeing his 40 time (Hunter skipped combine drills). Route-running ability on tape matters more than timed speed.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No strict threshold. Hunter ran 4.39 at pro day. Nacua ran 4.45. Kupp ran 4.62. Speed is nice but not the primary evaluation criterion.

playing_style.primary_style: Complete receiver with elite ball skills, route-running ability, and YAC potential. Not a one-dimensional deep threat — a versatile playmaker who wins at all levels of the field.
most_valued_traits: Ball skills and contested catch ability (Hunter and Nacua both elite here)
Route-running technique and ability to create separation
YAC ability and explosiveness after the catch
College production — Rams consistently draft productive college receivers
Versatility and ability to line up in multiple alignments
least_important_traits: Elite 40 time — Kupp ran 4.62 and was a Hall-of-Fame caliber pick. Speed is not the primary criterion.
deal_breakers: Lack of college production — Rams WR hits were all prolific college producers
Players who rely solely on physical traits without demonstrated route-running ability
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite college production combined with strong ball skills, even from smaller schools. Nacua was a 5th-round pick from BYU who set rookie records. Kupp was from Eastern Washington.

college_production.production_floor: Hunter: 96 catches, 1,258 yards, 15 TDs in 2024. Nacua: 48 catches in 2022 (BYU). Kupp: 117 catches in final year at Eastern Washington. High production is the common thread for premium WR investments.
college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending production valued. Hunter went from 57 catches to 96 catches. Nacua was more modest in college but dominated in other ways on film.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Very high — Rams/Gladstone famously mine non-power-5 schools. Nacua from BYU, Kupp from Eastern Washington. They evaluate production relative to opportunity and competition.

examples: 2025 Rd 1 (2) — Travis Hunter, Colorado: 6-0, 188, 4.39 40. 96 catches, 1,258 yards, 15 TDs in 2024 as WR. Also the draft's best CB. Heisman winner. Gladstone called him someone who can 'alter the trajectory of the sport.'
rams_lineage_examples: 2023 Rd 5 (177) — Puka Nacua, BYU: 6-2, 203. Set NFL rookie records with 1,486 yards and 105 catches. The quintessential Rams late-round WR find.
2017 Rd 3 (69) — Cooper Kupp, Eastern Washington: 6-2, 208, 4.62 40. Super Bowl LVI MVP. 3rd in franchise history in receptions. Gladstone was in his first year with the Rams when Kupp was drafted.

WR.confidence_note: 1 WR drafted (Hunter, who is also a CB). Rams lineage provides the strongest signal — Nacua and Kupp are the archetypes.

TE.archetype_summary: Receiving-first tight end with athleticism and developing blocking ability. Rams under Snead/Gladstone drafted Tyler Higbee (Rd 4, 2016, Western Kentucky) who became a productive receiving TE and Davis Allen (Rd 5, 2023, Clemson) as a rotational piece. The 2025 Rams drafted Terrance Ferguson (Rd 2, Oregon, 6-5, 247, 4.63) — a seam-running receiving TE who uses size at the catch point but needs to develop as a blocker. Gladstone did not draft a TE in 2025 for JAX but the Rams pattern suggests a preference for athletic, receiving-capable TEs.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Receiving ability and ability to separate. Blocking is developmental — the Rams have shown willingness to draft TEs who are better receivers than blockers and develop the blocking.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.60-4.75 range acceptable for Day 2-3 TEs based on Rams history

playing_style.primary_style: Receiving tight end who can threaten the seam and operate as a mismatch weapon in the passing game. Blocking ability is a developmental trait rather than a prerequisite.
most_valued_traits: Receiving ability and route-running from the TE position
Size and catch-point skills to win in contested situations
Ability to create mismatches against linebackers and safeties
Athletic profile — enough speed and agility to threaten seams
College production as a pass catcher
least_important_traits: Elite inline blocking — Rams have shown willingness to develop this trait over time
deal_breakers: Pure blocking TEs with no receiving upside
TEs with poor hands or ball skills
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite receiving production combined with functional athleticism

college_production.production_floor: Ferguson (Rams 2025): 134 career receptions, 1,537 yards, 16 TDs (school TE records). High production expected for premium TE investments.

rams_lineage_examples: 2025 Rd 2 (46) — Terrance Ferguson, Oregon: 6-5, 247, 4.63 40. Set school TE records. Seam-running receiver. Developing blocker.
2016 Rd 4 (110) — Tyler Higbee, Western Kentucky: 312 career receptions for Rams. Productive receiving TE.
2023 Rd 5 (175) — Davis Allen, Clemson: Rotational receiving TE.

TE.confidence_note: No TEs drafted by Gladstone for JAX. Entirely Rams lineage-derived. Jaguars have Brenton Strange under contract but TE depth is thin.

RB.archetype_summary: Dynamic, versatile backs with elite speed/explosiveness AND receiving ability. Two RBs drafted in 2025: Bhayshul Tuten (Rd 4, Virginia Tech) is a speed/explosiveness archetype — fastest RB at 2025 combine (4.32 40) with kick return ability and slashing running style. LeQuint Allen Jr. (Rd 7, Syracuse) is a receiving-back archetype — led all FBS RBs with 64 catches in 2024, pass-protection ability, 3rd-down specialist. Together they show Gladstone values dual-purpose backs who bring special teams/receiving value, not pure bellcow rushers. Rams lineage: Kyren Williams (Rd 5, 2022, Notre Dame) — undersized, all-purpose back who became a 2nd-team All-Pro.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: Not a primary factor for RBs
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed (Tuten: 4.32 40 — fastest in class) and explosiveness matter. Agility for pass-catching ability also valued.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Elite speed valued — Tuten's 4.32 was a specific draw. Allen's receiving ability compensates for less elite speed.

playing_style.primary_style: Versatile, multi-purpose back who can contribute as a runner, receiver, and kick returner. Not a bellcow-or-bust approach — values role-specific RBs who bring multiple dimensions.
most_valued_traits: Speed and explosiveness (Tuten: 4.32, kick return TDs)
Receiving ability and pass-catching hands (Allen: 64 catches, 521 yards, 4 TDs in 2024)
Special teams/kick return value (Tuten had 2 KR TDs in 2023)
Pass protection ability (Allen praised for blocking at Syracuse)
Zone-scheme fit — slashing, one-cut style for Coen's zone-run offense
least_important_traits: Pure between-the-tackles power — not the primary evaluation criterion
deal_breakers: Ball security issues — Tuten fumbled 9 times over two seasons and this was flagged as a concern
One-dimensional backs who can only run or only catch
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite speed combined with kick return ability — Tuten was drafted partly for his return game

college_production.production_floor: Tuten: 2,963 all-purpose yards over final two seasons. Allen: 1,021 rushing yards and 64 catches in 2024. Both were productive. No minimum rushing total appears required — receiving and return production count.
college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending production. Tuten improved from NC A&T to Virginia Tech. Allen was a multi-year starter at Syracuse with ascending catch totals.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Tuten came from NC A&T originally (FCS) before transferring to Virginia Tech. Gladstone/Rams are comfortable with players from smaller schools who proved it at higher levels.

examples: 2025 Rd 4 (104) — Bhayshul Tuten, Virginia Tech: ~5-10, ~200, 4.32 40 (fastest RB in 2025 class). 2,963 all-purpose yards over 2 seasons. 2 KR TDs. Slashing runner with explosive speed. Ball security concern (9 fumbles).

2025 Rd 7 (236) — LeQuint Allen Jr., Syracuse: ~5-10, ~210. Led FBS RBs with 64 catches in 2024. 1,021 rushing yards, 16 rushing TDs. Pass-catching specialist with blocking ability. 3rd-down back projection.

RB.confidence_note: 2 RBs drafted. Clear diversified approach: one speed/explosiveness back and one receiving/versatility back. Consistent with Rams mid-late round RB approach.

reach_tolerance.average_reach_picks: Unable to calculate precisely — only one draft. Hunter at No. 2 was consensus top-2, not a reach on player value (massive overpay on TRADE value). Day 2-3 picks appeared largely in range per consensus.
reach_tolerance.round_1_reach_tolerance: Low on player value — Hunter was consensus No. 1-2 player. High willingness to overpay in TRADE capital to move up for target player.
reach_tolerance.notes: Gladstone showed willingness to pay a massive premium to move up (gave up 2026 1st, 2025 2nd, 2025 4th to move from 5 to 2). This is 'reach' in trade capital rather than player value. The Rams under Snead routinely overpaid by trade chart to get targeted players. Gladstone likely carries this same willingness.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: McVay/Coen-influenced system: West Coast concepts with zone-run foundation, heavy play-action, motion-based, emphasis on YAC and misdirection. Coen ran this with Tampa Bay (top-5 in yards, scoring, passing, rushing in 2024). Requires: versatile OL who can zone-block, receiving RBs, WRs who win after the catch, athletic TEs. OC Grant Udinski comes from Minnesota Vikings system.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: Campanile's 4-3 multiple scheme with zone-coverage emphasis. Moved away from previous regime's press-heavy approach. Quarters/zone-shell coverage, rush four and play coverage. Values versatile DBs who can tackle and play zone. Needs disruptive DTs who can generate interior pressure with four rushers. EDGE rushers (Josh Hines-Allen, Travon Walker) are the established starters.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: All three coordinators (Udinski, Campanile, Farwell) returning for 2026. Scheme stability confirmed. This is year 2 of the system, so player acquisition should be more refined for scheme-specific fits.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Aggressive and bold. Gladstone's very first act as a GM was trading up in the top 5 of the draft, surrendering a future first-round pick for a unique two-way player — the first time a team had traded into the top 2 for a non-QB since the Rams moved up for Orlando Pace in 1997. He explicitly said 'don't be scared' and referenced the Rams' 'F--- Them Picks' philosophy. He is willing to take calculated risks for players he views as franchise-altering.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Moderate. Gladstone addressed the Jaguars' worst pass defense in the NFL (2024) by drafting Hunter (CB/WR) and Ransaw (DB). But Hunter was also consensus BPA, so it's hard to isolate recency bias from sound evaluation. The 2026 focus on DT and secondary depth following 2025 playoff loss also tracks with addressing demonstrated weaknesses.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Willing to move on from predecessor's picks. Parted ways with assistant GM Ethan Waugh and director of player personnel after his first draft. PFF noted that Gladstone's front office 'unlikely feels obligated to see Ezra Cleveland's contract through' (previous regime acquisition). Drafted Milum as competition/replacement. This suggests willingness to cut ties with inherited players who don't fit.
psychological_profile.combine_description: Very low combine weight. Gladstone skipped the 2026 combine in person, following the Rams' model of not attending since 2019. He stated all data is available remotely. He drafted Hunter without seeing his 40 time (Hunter skipped combine drills). Gladstone explicitly warned against 'implicit bias that can come to life' late in the process. He trusts film and sourced intel over short interactions and combine testing. Stated: 'Forget 40 times, height, weight, or arm length — look at Production Score' was how his approach was characterized by insiders.
psychological_profile.character_description: Extremely high. Gladstone's core mantra is drafting 'intangibly rich' players. He said: 'We will prioritize people and players that are intangibly rich and, by doing so, they will elevate our ecosystem.' Every 2025 draft pick was praised for character: Hunter (community leader), Kiser (Notre Dame Man of the Year, Wuerffel Trophy finalist), Lane (Navy player, special teams warrior). The draft was described as 'built on character and speed.' Gladstone does not host pre-draft facility visits (top-30 visits), reasoning that 'the sourced intel from those who have lived with these individuals is likely to be more accurate than me sitting down with a prospect for a short period of time.'
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Not relevant in current context — franchise QB Trevor Lawrence is under long-term contract. Gladstone has not addressed QB in the draft. His focus is on building around Lawrence.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Strongly leans toward production. Insiders characterized his approach as: 'If you want to know who the Jaguars are going to draft, look at Production Score.' Rams lineage backs this up — their biggest hits (Kupp, Nacua, Turner, Verse) were all highly productive college players. Gladstone values players who proved it on the field. The Rams' 'intangibly rich' philosophy partly means players whose production validated their talent.

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: Hunter had 96 catches, 1,258 yards, 15 TDs. Nacua/Kupp were prolific producers. Expect high production floor for WR investment.
production_thresholds_by_position.RB: Tuten had 2,963 all-purpose yards over 2 years. Allen had 64 catches plus 1,000 rushing yards. Dual-threat production valued.
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: McLeod had 22.5 career sacks. Rams EDGEs (Verse, Young) all had significant sack production. Minimum 5+ sacks in final season for early investment.
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: No clear statistical threshold — Hunter's production was elite on both sides. Ransaw's tackling and forced incompletion rate mattered more than raw INT totals.
production_thresholds_by_position.LB: Kiser had 70 career games and 90 tackles in final year. Experience and durability metrics matter.
production_thresholds_by_position.OL: Multi-year starters preferred. Milum was a 4-year starter and Big 12 OL of the Year.

college_production_preferences.notes: Gladstone values production in context — he'll draft a player from NC A&T who transferred and produced at Virginia Tech (Tuten) or from BYU who set rookie records (Nacua). Raw production numbers matter but are evaluated relative to opportunity, conference strength, and role. Transfer success stories appear to carry positive signal.

CB.notes: Hunter skipped combine drills and was still the No. 2 pick. Gladstone drafted him without seeing his 40 time. Film-first, no hard athletic cutoffs.

EDGE.notes: Rams drafted 6-1 Josaiah Stewart in 2025, showing willingness to break size norms. Motor and production matter more than measurables.

WR.notes: Cooper Kupp ran 4.62 and was arguably the best WR in the NFL for several years. Gladstone's Rams system does not have a speed floor for WRs.

RB.speed_preference: Elite speed valued — Tuten's 4.32 was fastest in class
RB.notes: Speed appears to be a differentiator for RB evaluation, unlike other positions. But Allen (Rd 7) wasn't a speed freak — he was a receiver/blocker.

OT.notes: Milum was evaluated based on Senior Bowl tape, not combine metrics.

DT.weight_preference: Under 300 lbs acceptable — all 5 recent Rams DT picks were under 300
DT.notes: Rams/Gladstone value quickness over size at DT. Willing to draft undersized penetrators.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Almost no hard cutoffs at any position. Film-first evaluation with combine data used as confirmatory information, not as eliminators. The Rams have consistently shown willingness to break conventional size/speed norms.

size_preferences_by_position.DT: Prefers undersized, quick penetrators (275-300 range based on Rams lineage)
size_preferences_by_position.EDGE: Flexible on size — from 6-1 to 6-4 based on Rams recent picks
size_preferences_by_position.WR: 5-11 to 6-2, moderate size preferred but not rigid
size_preferences_by_position.CB: 5-11 to 6-1, versatility matters more than ideal size

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: None specifically overweighted. The Rams/Gladstone evaluation process reviews combine data remotely and uses it to confirm film evaluation, not as primary decision drivers. Gladstone's approach was characterized as 'forget 40 times, height, weight, or arm length.'
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Overwhelmingly film-first. Gladstone drafted Travis Hunter without seeing his 40 time. He skipped the 2026 combine in person. He declined to host pre-draft facility visits. He said 'the sourced intel from those who have lived with these individuals is likely to be more accurate than me sitting down with a prospect.' When a player tests poorly but has great film, Gladstone appears to trust the film. When a player tests well but has poor film, Gladstone appears skeptical.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Swing for the fences on franchise-altering talent. Gladstone's only Round 1 pick was trading up to No. 2 for a generational player. He is willing to mortgage future draft capital for a player he views as transformational. In 2026, the Jaguars have NO first-round pick (traded to Cleveland). This makes Round 2 their de facto first pick.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: Primary roster-building rounds. In 2025, Gladstone used his Round 3 picks to address clear needs (secondary with Ransaw, OL with Milum). He also traded down to accumulate additional Round 3 picks for 2026. In 2026, with no 1st-round pick, Rounds 2-3 (4 picks in top 100) are critical. Expect need-driven picks with scheme-fit emphasis at these positions: DT, LB, CB, possibly EDGE depth.
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Developmental athletes, special teams contributors, and high-character depth pieces. Gladstone used Day 3 for: speed RB/kick returner (Tuten, Rd 4), special teams LB (Kiser, Rd 4), EDGE/LB tweener (McLeod, Rd 6), special teams safety (Lane, Rd 6), backup center (Monheim, Rd 7), and receiving RB (Allen, Rd 7). Clear Day 3 priorities: special teams value, positional versatility, character, and complementary skill sets that fill specific roster roles.
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: Unknown — only one draft. Jaguars are expected to receive compensatory picks in 2026 based on 2025 free agency departures.

hit_rate_by_position.notes: Impossible to assess hit rate with only one draft class, most of whom dealt with injuries. Hunter played 7 games before knee injury. Ransaw and McLeod missed the season on IR. Milum was limited. Tuten and Allen were the most productive rookies. Kiser contributed on special teams.

blind_spots: Too early to identify — need 2+ drafts
strengths: Rams draft classes under Gladstone's scouting (2022-2024) produced: Kyren Williams (2nd-team All-Pro RB), Puka Nacua (All-Pro WR, rookie records), Kobie Turner (All-Rookie DT), Byron Young (All-Rookie LB), Steve Avila (All-Rookie IOL), Jared Verse (Defensive ROY EDGE), Braden Fiske (DROY nominee DT). This suggests strong evaluation of defensive front players and offensive playmakers.

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Aggressive roster overhaul in first offseason. Signed multiple OL starters (Patrick Mekari, Robert Hainsey), secondary pieces (Jourdan Lewis, Eric Murray), and depth players. Consistent with Rams philosophy articulated by Snead: 'Use free agency to not be desperate in the draft.' Gladstone made substantial FA investments to plug the most critical holes, then used the draft to add premium talent (Hunter) and developmental depth.
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When Gladstone signed 4 OL in free agency, he STILL drafted OL (Milum, Rd 3; Monheim, Rd 7) — showing he views OL depth as a never-ending investment. He said: 'You can never have enough security across the offensive line.' He does not stop drafting a position just because he addressed it in FA.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through a combination of aggressive FA, bold trades, and draft. Not a pure 'build through the draft' GM — his Rams background involved trading for Matthew Stafford, Von Miller, Jalen Ramsey. But post-Super Bowl, the Rams shifted to draft-and-develop, and Gladstone's 2025 approach reflects this hybrid: FA for established starters, draft for upside and depth. Gladstone emphasized having the 'highest volume of draft capital that exists for any team in the NFL' and being 'uniquely positioned to navigate' that volume.

notable_patterns: Traded UP in Round 1 for generational talent and traded DOWN on Day 2 to accumulate future picks — dual approach of aggressive targeting and capital management
4 of 9 picks were defensive players, 4 were offensive, 1 was a two-way player (Hunter). Balanced approach.
Every single pick was praised for character, work ethic, and intangibles — 'intangibly rich' is the defining evaluation criterion
Did not host pre-draft top-30 visits — trusts film and sourced intel over short meetings
Film-first evaluation: drafted Hunter without seeing his combine testing, skipped 2026 combine in person
Values positional versatility: Hunter (WR/CB), Ransaw (CB/S/nickel), Milum (OT/OG), McLeod (LB/EDGE), Lane (S/ST), Allen (RB/receiver)
Special teams value prioritized on Day 3: Tuten (kick returner), Kiser (special teams standout), Lane (gunner/special teams warrior)

biggest_reaches.reach_amount: N/A — player was consensus No. 1-2. The 'reach' was in trade capital (2026 1st, 2025 2nd, 2025 4th to move from 5 to 2), not in player evaluation.
biggest_reaches.context: Gladstone said the deal was 'more than likely going to be a stretch' but the Jaguars and Browns reached agreement 2 weeks before the draft.

biggest_steals.context: Allen led FBS RBs with 64 catches. His pass-catching and pass-protection skills translated immediately — he got more playing time than the higher-drafted Tuten.

key_needs: DT — aging starters (Armstead, Hamilton), Maason Smith hasn't developed. Interior disruption needed.
CB — Montaric Brown and Greg Newsome II expected to hit free agency. Hunter shifting to more CB in 2026 helps but depth needed.
LB — Devin Lloyd (2nd-team All-Pro) expected to leave in free agency. Major need.
S — Andrew Wingard hitting free agency. Ransaw returning from injury.
WR — Brian Thomas Jr. trade rumors. Dyami Brown was a 'major flop.' Hunter's shift to more defense reduces offensive WR snaps.
EDGE depth — Need a reliable 3rd edge rusher behind Hines-Allen and Walker.
current_2026_draft_context.draft_capital_philosophy_for_2026: Gladstone is 'uniquely positioned to navigate' a draft without a first-round pick based on his Rams experience (7 consecutive years without one). He has shown willingness to trade up using Day 2/3 capital. Expect him to be aggressive with his 4 top-100 picks and potentially trade up into Round 1 if a target falls.

confidence_notes: Only 1 draft as Jacksonville GM. Rams scouting tree is the primary behavioral signal. Gladstone's 2025 season was a resounding success (4 wins to 13, AFC South champions) which validates some early approaches but doesn't provide enough draft-specific data for high confidence. Profile heavily weighted toward Rams lineage and stated philosophy. Must be refreshed after 2026 draft.
